        # Verify required scope
        if "api_read_access" not in payload.get("ias_apis", []):
            raise Exception("Missing required ias_apis scope")

        return payload

    async def dispatch(self, request: Request, call_next):
        # Extract and validate authorization header
        auth_header = request.headers.get("Authorization")

        if not auth_header or not auth_header.startswith("Bearer "):
            return JSONResponse(
                status_code=401,
                content={"detail": "Missing or invalid authorization header"}
            )

        token = auth_header.split(" ")[1]

        try:
            payload = self.verify_token(token)
            request.state.user = payload
        except Exception as e:
            return JSONResponse(
                status_code=401,
                content={"detail": f"Authentication failed: {str(e)}"}
            )

        return await call_next(request)


@mcp.tool()
def research(query: str) -> str:
    """
    Research tool using Perplexity AI with real-time web search and citations.

    Args:
        query: The research query

    Returns:
        Research results with answer and source citations
    """
    return client.research(query)


# Create ASGI app from MCP server
mcp_app = mcp.http_app(path='/mcp')

# Create FastAPI wrapper with lifespan management
app = FastAPI(title="Researcher MCP", lifespan=mcp_app.lifespan)

# Add IAS authentication middleware
mcp_app.add_middleware(IASAuthMiddleware)

# Mount the MCP server
app.mount("/", mcp_app)


if __name__ == "__main__":
    uvicorn.run(app, host="0.0.0.0", port=int(os.environ.get("PORT", 8080)))

What changed for authentication:

The key difference from the unsecured version is the middleware layer. Since fastmcp is built on Starlette/FastAPI, we can leverage standard ASGI middleware patterns. The IASAuthMiddleware intercepts every request, validates the JWT token against IAS's public keys (JWKS), and checks required claims before allowing access to MCP endpoints.

To integrate the middleware, we extract the ASGI app from fastmcp using http_app(), wrap it with FastAPI to manage lifespan properly, add our authentication middleware, and mount it. This pattern works seamlessly with Cloud Foundry's router.

Testing Authentication

Test your secured endpoint:

1. Without token (should fail):
curl "https://research-mcp-server-ias-auth.cfapps.sap.hana.ondemand.com/mcp"
Response: 401 - {"detail":"Missing or invalid authorization header"}

2. With valid token (should succeed):
curl "https://research-mcp-server-ias-auth.cfapps.sap.hana.ondemand.com/mcp" \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer eyJqa3U..."
Response: 200 OK with mcp-session-id header

Perfect! Our MCP server now requires valid IAS authentication.

Using the MCP Server in Joule Studio

Now that we have a secured MCP server, let's integrate it with Joule Studio to build an AI agent with research capabilities.

Step 1: Create a Destination

Navigate to your BTP Subaccount (where Joule Studio is running):
1. Go to Connectivity → Destinations
2. Create a new destination with these settings:
- Name: research-mcp-server
- Type: HTTP
- URL: https://research-mcp-server-ias-auth.cfapps.sap.hana.ondemand.com
- Authentication: OAuth2ClientCredentials
- Client ID: Your IAS client ID
- Client Secret: Your IAS client secret
- Token Service URL: https://your-tenant.accounts.ondemand.com/oauth2/token

Additional Properties are needed for Joule Studio to recognize the Destination accordingly:

sap-joule-studio-mcp-server: true
sap.processautomation.enabled: true

Important: Don't include /mcp in the URL — Joule Studio appends this automatically.

Now the Destination again needs to be added to the respective environment of SAP Build Process Automation via the Control Tower > Destinations.


Step 2: Create Agent in Joule Studio

 

1. Create a new Agent in your Joule Extension Project
2. Define the agent's expertise and instructions
3. In the MCP section, click Add MCP Server
4. Select your research-mcp-server destination
5. Verify the /mcp path (default is fine)
6. You'll see the available tools (in our case: research)

 


Step 3: Test Your Agent

Start a conversation with your agent and ask it to research current information:

"Research the current situation in the Middle East"

The agent now has access to real-time web information via your MCP server!

 


This opens powerful possibilities: combine up-to-date web data with transactional business data from S/4HANA, SuccessFactors, or other SAP systems.

Using It in Action

Now let's test it in action - I started the agent and asked it to research the current situation in the Middle East and it works! Up-to-date information directly in Joule - can now be combined with any business transactional data one might pull from an S/4HANA system.

By the way, check out my blog on connecting Joule Studio to on-premises HTTP APIs to extend this pattern further.

In the GitHub repository I've added all the different examples including the simple example, with auth based on IAS as well as based on XSUAA, and the Perplexity code. Hope you found this interesting. Leave a comment in case of any questions!

entry.title: My AI Experience Sharing – Setting up Joule for the S/4HANA Public Edition (Pt D: Improvements)
entry.content:
Introduction

Now Artificial Intelligence (AI) is on every professional’s mind. Everybody wants to be on the bandwagon.  I plan to write a series of blogs on this topic under a big umbrella “My AI Experience Sharing”.  This is the first mini-series: setting up Joule for the SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition. 

I purposely delayed my action on setting up a Joule to wait for the dust settling down during the Early Adopter phase. The intention paid off but still won’t give me a free pass 😉. There are five credible references available as listed in the Reference section, but none of them give you a good explanation on Why, especially on What in a critical step with a readable picture when you desperately need a hint.  To help all the people like me, this blog will focus on explaining those steps, what to enter, and possible mistakes to avoid. 

This week, I re-did the Joule Setup on a Parallel Line of the SAP S/4HANA ERP Public Edition, aka Public Cloud. I noticed a few changes and would like to outline here for your reference:

You can install Joule on your Parallel Line

Several weeks ago, I met one of our customers at the Customer Success Day in NYC. After listening to his rational behind requiring Joule on the Parallel Line, I promised to do a test. I did just that this week on a Parallel Line. It worked.

No need to NavigationService

I heard there is an improvement in the Setup process, i.e., no more need to manually create the NavigationService destination (outlined in Step B.2.4 of my blog My AI Experience Sharing – Setting up Joule for the S/4HANA Public Edition (Pt B:Joule Installation). If you continue to add this destination, it won’t hurt because the system is no longer seeking the information stored there. I will remove this step in my blog.

Joule Booster is Faster

The Joule Booster is noticeably faster.  In my case, it is about 15 minutes to complete the running.

SAP Work Zone Content Provider

When I provision the user and group information from the Public Cloud to the Work Zone via IPS, I got an error: The provided providerId is illegal: "status":400,"scimType":"invalidValue".  There is even an KBA addressing this problem 3505749 - The provided providerId is illegal: "status":400,"scimType":"invalidValue".  It turns out not because of my ProviderID is bad, but I only copied part of the ProviderID 6d5e30cb-4 from sap.start.6d5e30cb-4.  I learned that from creating my own ProviderID.  The entire string is the ProviderID, not part of it.  The details count.

Joule Icon Not Shown (4/9/2026)

While installing Joule recently, I found the Joule Icon won't show on the Fiori Launchpad. By further investigation, I learned that from Release 2602, SP 06, Patch 0005, a new authorization is implemented: for Joule icon to be seen, a user needs a business catalog SAP_CORE_BC_UI_JOU_PC.  Here is the background information:

After running Joule Booster, two communication scenarios were created automatically:

  • SAP_COM_0647 for Launchpad Content Exposure to SAP BTP Integration
  • SAP_COM_0882 for Joule Integration

Within SAP_COM_0882, there is one property called Plug-in Activation State. This property controls how the Joule icon shows up or not, or who can use Joule or not.  It has three available values:

  • Empty - Joule is available to all users;
  • DISABLED - Joule is not available to users;
  • ROLE - Joule is only available to those users with the proper authorization, i.e., the users with the business catalog SAP_CORE_BC_UI_JOU_PC.

Property Plug-in Activation State

 With this new parameter, the Administrator can control who can access Joule or not.

The business catalog is part of the SAP_BR_EMPLOYEE business role template. 

If you use a business role copied from the SAP's role template SAP_BR_EMPLOYEE , you need to re-sync it to add this business catalog.

If you don't use this SAP_BR_EMPLOYEE template, just create a new business role to assign the business catalog SAP_CORE_BC_UI_JOU_PC to it. You can name this new business role like "Display Joule Icon", and assign all users to it.

There is an KBA discussing this topic: 3720220 - SAP Joule Icon Does Not Appear in the SAP S4 HANA Cloud Public Edition After Configuration Has Been Completed. For the Public Cloud users, it is recommended to select the value ROLE so that you have control the access to Joule.

 

References

  1. Blog 1: Joule – End-to-End Setup Guide (for all Line of Business) Unified Approach
  2. Blog 2: Joule in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition - Setup Guide
  3. Discovery Center 1: Establish a Unified Joule Instance
  4. Discovery Center 2: Activate Joule with SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition
  5. SAP Help: Joule onboarding guide
  6. My Blog: My AI Experience Sharing – Setting up Joule for the SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition (Part A Prepara...
  7. My Blog: My AI Experience Sharing – Setting up Joule for the SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition (Part B Joule I...
  8. My Blog: My AI Experience Sharing – Setting up Joule for the SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition (Part C Joule D...
  9. My Blog: My AI Experience Sharing – Setting up Joule for the SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition (Part D Improvements)

entry.title: Terraform in Practice: Setting Up Joule Studio
entry.content:
Introduction 

As Developer Advocates, we run SAP CodeJam events where participants need hands-on access to the Joule Studio environment, typically provided by us during the day of the event. The challenge: a Joule Studio tenant has limits on the number of deployments that can be made, and these limits are reached in a CodeJam setting where 30+ developers are simultaneously testing their skills and agents, pushing deployments etc, so each CodeJam event requires spinning up a brand new tenant from scratch knowing which subscription, instances, plan, role collections, destinations, and credentials to use while creating a new tenant. 

Doing these tasks by hand is a long and tedious process. It takes too many clicks and careful typing, which makes it easy to make a mistake. With CodeJam events happening frequently, this process was becoming a real bottleneck. Terraform seemed like a good choice at this point to perform these steps. Instead of repeating the same manual steps every time, we could automate the entire tenant provisioning process, turning what used to take significant manual effort into a few commands and getting a Joule Studio tenant at the end of the execution which typically takes 20 to 30 mins. 

I had the opportunity to explore Terraform during a hands-on session at SAP Inside Track, Bangalore in June 2025.  After trying it out in the developer hands on area, I could see its value for automating the creation and management of SAP BTP subaccounts. During Devtoberfest 2025, SAP experts on Terraform presented a session titled From Clicks to Code: Automate SAP BTP Setup with Terraform on how to set up a BTP environment with Terraform. While trying to learn more about Terraform, I also came across a learning.sap.com course that is comphrensive. The course is available here and the GitHub repository used in this learning course is available here. There are also some excellent posts in the SAP Community portal for beginners looking to get started with Terraform, the links to those posts can be found in the Additional References section. Rather than repeating what's already well-documented, I'll focus on the key concepts behind the Terraform and share a few things I picked up along the way and provide you a Terraform script to get started with Joule Studio setup.  

What is Terraform? 

Terraform uses HashiCorp Configuration Language for building infrastructure with configuration files. Terraform's configuration language is declarative, meaning that it describes the desired end state for your infrastructure. It also keeps track of your real infrastructure in a state file, which acts as a source of truth for your environment. Terraform uses the state file to determine the changes to make to your infrastructure so that it will match your configuration. Terraform builds a resource graph to determine resource dependencies and creates or modifies non-dependent resources in parallel. This allows Terraform to provision resources efficiently. Terraform plugins called providers lets Terraform interact with cloud platforms and other services via their application programming interfaces (APIs). SAP provides dedicated Terraform providers for BTP, Cloud Identity, Cloud Foundry, and Cloud Connector. The BTP provider functions by wrapping BTP CLI commands, allowing you to convert imperative CLI commands into declarative Terraform resources. See the registry for documentation on how to use them: https://registry.terraform.io/search/providers?namespace=SAP. 

Using Terraform as an Administrator 

There are two ways to run the terraform script to create a Joule Studio environment. We will look at how to run the Terraform scripts on a local machine with Terraform CLI first. The approach mentioned below requires minor changes depending on whether we are running it on a Mac or Windows machine. 

  1. Clone this repository https://github.com/SAP-samples/joule-studio-setup-terraform
  2. Open it in VS Code
  3. Go through the pre-requisites section in the README file 
  4. Install Terraform CLI https://developer.hashicorp.com/terraform/tutorials/aws-get-started/install-cli 
  5. Set up environment variables by running these commands on your VS Code terminal  

On Mac/Linux 

export TF_VAR_btp_username='your-btp-username' 
export TF_VAR_btp_password='your-btp-user-password'

On Windows (PowerShell) 

$env:TF_VAR_btp_username = 'your-btp-username' 
$env:TF_VAR_btp_password = 'your-btp-user-password'

6. Configure the values for global account subdomain, identity provider domain, subdomain name and region in the terraform.tfvars file

Project in VS Code 7. Go to the root directory and run the commands from your terminal one by one. 

terraform init
terraform apply --auto-approve

The terraform init command installs the SAP BTP and Cloud foundry providers configured in the main.tf file in the repository.  

The terraform apply command installs the entitlements and subscriptions mentioned in the .tf files. 

8. Once all the resources are created, run Joule booster to create formation to complete the Joule studio setup. See the steps here on running the Joule booster. 

9. Navigate to the Instances and Subscriptions page of your new BTP subaccount; ensure the following subscriptions and service instances have been successfully provisioned.

The second approach is explained in the GitHub Actions section. 

GitHub Actions 

Now, the local Terraform CLI approach works perfectly fine and well suited for a development workflow, but what if you would rather not install anything on your machine at all? 

That's where GitHub Actions comes in. GitHub Actions lets you automate anything, triggered either by an event in your repository (like a push or a PR) or kicked off manually straight from the GitHub UI. Your workflows live as YAML files inside the .github/workflows directory of your repository. 

Check out the README of this repository to follow this approach for creating your Joule Studio setup: https://github.com/SAP-samples/joule-studio-setup-terraform 

One thing worth knowing: you can override any terraform.tfvars variables defined in your repository with the corresponding values in the GitHub variables by passing them via the --var flag. You can see how to pass the variables in the terraform.yml file under .github/workflows of the repository. Anything you haven't explicitly overridden will simply fall back to whatever is defined in terraform.tfvars. 

Use this approach when you have a stable version of a script that can be used to create a new tenant with all the resources and dependencies. 

Using Terraform as a Developer 

My recommendation would be to stick to the local Terraform CLI approach while you are actively writing or updating your scripts. The reason is simple: when something fails during development, Terraform lets you fix just the broken parts and apply only those changes. That kind of control is needed when you are iterating quickly. 

Before you start customizing the script to fit your needs, there are also a handful of expressions and constructs worth getting accustomed to.  So, let’s walk through the significant ones. 

Variables 

All the variables used in the script are defined in the variables.tf file with an optional default value if needed. Configurable values such as global account name, sub account region etc can be provided in the terraform.tfvars file. Sensitive information like passwords and secrets should be stored as environment variables in local machines or as GitHub secrets in GitHub. Terraform makes this easy with the TF_VAR_ prefix. Any environment variable with that prefix gets automatically picked up as a Terraform input variable. So, if you set TF_VAR_btp_password in your environment, Terraform maps it directly to var.btp_password in your script. This keeps credentials in GitHub Secrets or Environment variables and passes them securely to Terraform without exposing them in logs or files. 

If a variable has no default value and you haven't provided it through .tfvars or an environment variable, Terraform will prompt you for it in the terminal during plan or apply. It won't silently fail, but it will interrupt your flow, so it's worth getting your variables sorted before you run anything.  

Built -in functions: 

There is a set of built-in functions available that you can use within your scripts from this reference. Some of the functions used in the repository are lower(), replace(), trim(), toset(), contains() etc. When a subaccount is created, the name of the subaccount subdomain needs to confirm certain rules defined by BTP. We could use these built-in functions to sanitize the user input provided in the terraform.tfvars file and create a subaccount subdomain that confirms those naming conventions.  

raw_subdomain = lower("${var.subaccount_name}-${var.subaccount_subdomain}-${var.subaccount_region}") 
subaccount_subdomain = trim (replace (replace (raw_subdomain, "/[^a-z0-9-]/", "-"), "/--+/", "-"), "-")

This code sanitizes and formats a string to create a subdomain by concatenating three variables into lowercase and using regular expressions to replace any invalid special characters or double-hyphens with a single dash.

Meta-argument 

depends_on is a meta-argument to define explicit dependency. For eg: if you want to create a subscription for Build Work Zone but the entitlement for Build Work Zone should exist before the subscription happens. In such cases, the depends_on argument can be used to ensure that the entitlement is created before the subscription. By default, Terraform executes resources in parallel.

local values block  

When you want to define a local variable or derive a value from a variable, you can use locals. For instance, the cf_api_url is derived from subaccount_region variable, so we can define it within local value block and then use them by referencing it as local.cf_api.url  

locals {
  cf_api_url = https://api.cf.${var.subaccount_region}.hana.ondemand.com 
}

Ternary operators 

Ternary operators can be used to apply conditional expressions. 

plan_name = var.environment == "prod" ? "standard" : "free"

If the environment is production, it assigns the value "standard" to plan_name.

What I learned about the Development workflow 

 After working with Terraform, here is what I consider to be the best practices to follow: 

  • Always start with terraform fmt -recursive before committing anything. It ensures your code style remains consistent and identifies basic syntax issues early, preventing them from causing problems later
  • Run terraform validate after every code change. It's quick, doesn't need credentials, and gives you instant feedback on syntax and data type errors 
  • Never skip the terraform plan -out=tfplan even for "small" changes. This step shows you exactly what Terraform is about to create, modify, or destroy. Review the output carefully before moving on
  • Then apply with terraform apply tfplan using the saved plan file. This guarantees you are applying exactly what you reviewed 
  • Avoid --auto-approve flags in production
  • Never commit .tfstate files as state files can contain sensitive data like passwords and secrets. Add this file to .gitignore. Use a secure remote state backend instead 
  • Always commit .terraform.lock.hcl. This locks your provider versions and ensures everyone on the team gets reproducible results

Further extension of the script 

Think of the script as your starting point, it gets you a working Joule Studio setup quickly. Depending on your scenario, you will likely want to extend it.  

For Joule to connect to SAP AI Core (for document grounding), an S3 bucket (for storing documents), an MCP Server (for access to live data) or external OData APIs via BTP destinations, you will need to add those destinations in Terraform. Create a new destination.tf file, add your destination configurations and commit it to the repository and execute the terraform cli commands.

One other thing worth mentioning is Cloud Identity Services. The script assumes your global account already has Cloud Identity Services set up, which is the case for most existing BTP accounts. If that is your case, all you need to do is provide the identity provider domain value in terraform.tfvars and you are good to go. 

However, if your account doesn't have Cloud Identity Services configured yet in the global account, you will need to add a resource for the entitlement and subscription as well. For that, I would point you to the Cloud Identity Services provider documentation, it covers exactly what you need to set that up. 

 Refer to the samples shown here for setting up destination and Cloud Identity Services

  •  https://registry.terraform.io/providers/SAP/btp/latest/docs/resources/subaccount_destination 
  • https://registry.terraform.io/providers/SAP/sap-cloud-identity-services/latest/docs 

 Additional References

  • https://github.com/SAP-samples/btp-terraform-samples 
  • https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-members/automating-sap-btp-setup-with-the-new-terraform-provider-for-sap-btp/ba-p/13549469 
  • https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/extending-infrastructure-as-code-for-sap-btp-managing-resources-on-sap-btp/ba-p/14062455 
  • https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-members/easy-way-to-automate-and-configure-the-setup-of-a-btp-account-and-cloud/ba-p/13677419

entry.title: Joule A2A: Secure Code Based Agents with IAS
entry.content:
In the previous blog post, we explored how to integrate a Pro-Code Agent into the Joule ecosystem. While the initial setup functioned correctly, it did not include proper authentication, leaving the agent accessible to anyone on the internet.

In this post, we address that gap by implementing secure access controls. Specifically, we will demonstrate how to protect your agent using SAP Cloud Identity Services – Identity Authentication Service (IAS) in a Python-based setup.

Scenario

Our target is to use SAP Cloud Identity Services - Identity Authentication Service (short: IAS) to take care of the authentication. We will implement the authentication check on the Python side using middleware that validates JWT tokens from IAS.

Important distinction: In this blog, we focus on protecting the agent from unauthorized access using the OAuth2 Client Credentials flow. This is app-to-app authentication - we verify that the calling application (e.g., Joule) has valid credentials to access our agent. The token identifies the application, not the end user.

If your agent needs to call backend systems on behalf of the actual user (e.g., accessing SAP S/4HANA with the user's permissions), you'll need Principal Propagation - forwarding the user's identity through the call chain. That's a different pattern which I'll cover in a follow-up blog post.

By the end of this blog, you will have a secure A2A agent that validates JWT tokens and IAS configured with the necessary applications and API scopes.


Prerequisites

For the IAS setup basics, please refer to my previous blog post: Using IAS to Secure Python APIs on Cloud Foundry. That post covers:

  • Creating an application in IAS
  • Configuring Provided APIs (scopes like api_read_access)
  • Creating client credentials with API permissions

So please follow the chapter "Creating an Application in IAS" closely and then get started here.

For this scenario, you will need:

  1. IAS Tenant: Access to an SAP Cloud Identity Services tenant with an application configured
  2. Client Credentials: Client ID and Client Secret with api_read_access scope
  3. BTP Cloud Foundry: A Cloud Foundry space to deploy the agent
  4. SAP AI Core: For the agent's LLM capabilities (optional)

The overall setup of the A2A Integration is explained in my previous blog post regarding A2A. We are building upon that one here.


Part 1: Understanding JWT Token Verification

Before diving into the code, let's understand what we're actually verifying. When a client authenticates with IAS using the client credentials flow, IAS returns a JWT (JSON Web Token). This token is a Base64-encoded JSON object with three parts: header, payload, and signature.

Here's what a decoded IAS token payload looks like:

{
  "ias_apis": [
    "api_read_access",
    "api_write_access"
  ],
  "sub": "<your-client-id>",
  "aud": "<your-client-id>",
  "sap_id_type": "app",
  "azp": "<your-client-id>",
  "iss": "https://your-tenant.accounts.ondemand.com",
  "exp": 1775566673,
  "iat": 1775563073,
  "jti": "0324db00-39ab-4dc5-888c-11fd4959aef5"
}

The key fields our middleware needs to verify are: iss (issuer) confirms the token came from our IAS tenant, aud (audience) confirms the token is meant for our application, exp (expiration) ensures the token hasn't expired, and ias_apis contains the scopes we configured in IAS - this is where we check for api_read_access.

The token is cryptographically signed by IAS. To verify this signature, our middleware fetches the public keys from IAS's JWKS (JSON Web Key Set) endpoint at /oauth2/certs. This ensures nobody can forge a valid token - only IAS can create tokens that pass verification.


Part 2: The IAS Authentication Middleware

Now let's look at the implementation. The middleware is intentionally minimal - about 50 lines of code:

"""IAS Authentication Middleware for A2A Server."""

import os

import jwt
import requests
from starlette.middleware.base import BaseHTTPMiddleware
from starlette.requests import Request
from starlette.responses import JSONResponse

# Config - Load from environment variables
ISSUER = os.environ.get("IAS_ISSUER", "https://your-tenant.accounts.ondemand.com")
JWKS_URL = f"{ISSUER}/oauth2/certs"
AUDIENCE = os.environ.get("IAS_AUDIENCE", "your-client-id-here")


class IASAuthMiddleware(BaseHTTPMiddleware):
    """Validates JWT tokens from SAP IAS."""

    def get_public_key(self, token: str):
        """Fetch JWKS and find the matching public key."""
        kid = jwt.get_unverified_header(token)["kid"]
        jwks = requests.get(JWKS_URL).json()

        for key in jwks["keys"]:
            if key["kid"] == kid:
                return jwt.algorithms.RSAAlgorithm.from_jwk(key)

        raise Exception("No matching key found")

    def verify_token(self, token: str):
        """Validate JWT token and return payload."""
        public_key = self.get_public_key(token)
        payload = jwt.decode(
            token, public_key, algorithms=["RS256"],
            audience=AUDIENCE, issuer=ISSUER
        )

        if "api_read_access" not in payload.get("ias_apis", []):
            raise Exception("Missing required ias_apis scope")

        return payload

    async def dispatch(self, request: Request, call_next):
        # Allow public discovery endpoints
        if request.url.path.startswith("/.well-known/"):
            return await call_next(request)

        auth_header = request.headers.get("Authorization")

        if not auth_header or not auth_header.startswith("Bearer "):
            return JSONResponse(status_code=401, content={"detail": "Missing token"})

        token = auth_header.split(" ")[1]

        try:
            payload = self.verify_token(token)
            request.state.user = payload
        except Exception as e:
            return JSONResponse(status_code=401, content={"detail": str(e)})

        return await call_next(request)

The get_public_key method fetches the JWKS from IAS and finds the key matching the token's kid (key ID) header. The verify_token method uses PyJWT to decode and verify the token in one step - it checks the signature, issuer, audience, and expiration automatically. We then add our custom check for the api_read_access scope in the ias_apis claim. The dispatch method is called for every request and either lets it through (for /.well-known/ paths) or validates the Bearer token.

Note: Some endpoint are meant to be public - /.well-known/ contains the agent cards and is used for the discovery. Thus we want to exclude that from the auth checks.


Part 3: Integrating with the A2A Server

Adding the middleware to your A2A server is straightforward - just one line:

from a2a.server.apps import A2AStarletteApplication
from middleware.ias_auth import IASAuthMiddleware

# ... setup agent_card and request_handler ...

server = A2AStarletteApplication(
    agent_card=agent_card, http_handler=request_handler
)

# Build app and add IAS auth middleware
app = server.build()
app.add_middleware(IASAuthMiddleware)


Deployment Configuration

Set the environment variables in your manifest.yaml:

applications:
- name: currency-agent
  memory: 512M
  buildpack: python_buildpack
  command: uvicorn app:app --host 0.0.0.0 --port ${PORT}
  env:
    IAS_ISSUER: "https://your-tenant.accounts.ondemand.com"
    IAS_AUDIENCE: "your-client-id"

The IAS_ISSUER is relating to your IAS tenant URL. The IAS_AUDIENCE to the respective client. In our setup this matches to the client_id of the application we maintained in IAS.

Deploy with:

cd app
cf push


Part 4: Testing the Authentication

Let's verify that our authentication is working correctly.


Step 1: Test Public Endpoint (No Auth Required)

The agent card is accessible without authentication for A2A discovery:

curl -s "https://<your-agent>.cfapps.sap.hana.ondemand.com/.well-known/agent.json" | jq '.'

Response:

{
  "name": "Currency Agent",
  "description": "Helps with exchange rates for currencies",
  "version": "1.0.0",
  "protocolVersion": "0.3.0",
  "capabilities": {
    "pushNotifications": true,
    "streaming": true
  },
  "skills": [
    {
      "id": "convert_currency",
      "name": "Currency Exchange Rates Tool",
      "description": "Helps with exchange values between various currencies"
    }
  ]
}


Step 2: Test Without Token (Should Fail)

We use the JSON-RPC syntax for doing a HTTP request - sending a basic message construct.

curl -s "https://<your-agent>.cfapps.sap.hana.ondemand.com/" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"jsonrpc": "2.0", "method": "message/send", "params": {}, "id": "1"}'

Response:

As expected - we get the HTTP 401 - Missing Token Error we implemented.

{
  "detail": "Missing token"
}


Step 3: Get Token and Call Agent

Using our IAS Tenant URL to retrieve a Token giving our client credentials. Then doing an actual message send via the A2A syntax. This time we actually include some text message.

# Get token from IAS
TOKEN=$(curl -s -X POST "https://<tenant>.accounts.ondemand.com/oauth2/token" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded" \
  -d "grant_type=client_credentials" \
  -d "client_id=<client-id>" \
  -d "client_secret=<client-secret>" | jq -r '.access_token')

# Call the agent
curl -s "https://<your-agent>.cfapps.sap.hana.ondemand.com/" \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer $TOKEN" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{
    "jsonrpc": "2.0",
    "method": "message/send",
    "params": {
      "message": {
        "role": "user",
        "parts": [{"kind": "text", "text": "What is 100 USD in EUR?"}],
        "messageId": "msg-123"
      }
    },
    "id": "1"
  }' | jq '.'

Response:

{
  "id": "1",
  "jsonrpc": "2.0",
  "result": {
    "artifacts": [
      {
        "artifactId": "ad8b315f-dd87-45ca-9f5e-584439102732",
        "name": "conversion_result",
        "parts": [
          {
            "kind": "text",
            "text": "100 USD is approximately 86.77 EUR based on the current exchange rate."
          }
        ]
      }
    ],
    "contextId": "095d62bd-dfa1-4b77-991b-392fe7038ecd",
    "id": "639c1a4b-d486-45df-b4be-4b614e0c441d",
    "kind": "task",
    "status": {
      "state": "completed",
      "timestamp": "2026-04-07T11:44:14.740338+00:00"
    }
  }
}

And as a result we actually get the converted currency - presenting the auth header!

Part 5: Connecting to Joule

Now that your agent is secured with IAS authentication, you need to configure Joule to communicate with it via a BTP Destination. Last time we configured the Destination without any authentication. This time we opt for OAuth2ClientCredentials.

Create the BTP Destination

In your BTP Cockpit, create a destination for your secured agent:

1. Navigate to Connectivity > Destinations
2. Create a new destination with these settings:
- Name: CURRENCY_AGENT
- Type: HTTP
- URL: https://<your-agent>.cfapps.sap.hana.ondemand.com
- Proxy Type: Internet
- Authentication: OAuth2ClientCredentials
- Client ID: <Your IAS Client ID>
- Client Secret: <Your IAS Client Secret>
- Token Service URL: https://your-tenant.accounts.ondemand.com/oauth2/token

Note: Since this blog builds upon the last one - ensure that you have already created a cabability in Joule that points a agent-request towards this destination. Once you created / updated this destination - make sure to update the Joule Capability. Because Joule is apparently caching the Destination content for some time. You can use joule delete and joule deploy to do so!

Test from Joule

Once the destination is configured, you can interact with the secured agent through Joule:

 


Conclusion

With just ~50 lines of middleware code, we've secured our A2A agent with IAS authentication. The middleware validates JWT tokens from IAS, checks the required scopes, and keeps the agent discovery endpoints public for A2A protocol compliance. The full code is available on GitHub.

I hope you found this insightful. Feel free to leave questions in the comments!

entry.title: IPS (Identity Provisioning) to the IAS mirgation
entry.content:
How Do I know If I’m using the old IAS admin tool?

Just the fact that you are able to access to your IPS cockpit/ platform, means you have to do the migration.

Other way, is to verify your current menu in the IAS admin panel. ou will see a Menu of 3 items like the below:

 

Migration Wizard

A wizard guides you through the migration process.

First, you choose the target IAS tenant to which you want to migrate your Identity Provisioning tenant.

Then, you select the provisioning systems – source, target and proxy, regardless of their status (enabled or disabled).

 
The process moves the system configurations, such as properties, destinations, credentials and transformations, to the tenant in the new infrastructure. Provisioning job logs are not migrated. They are retained in your Neo tenant according to the period of time (7, 14 or 30 days) you have configured. The migration job log is retained for 30 days.

 

SAP Help about IPS migration to IAShttps://help.sap.com/docs/identity-provisioning/identity-provisioning/migrate-identity-provisioning-bundle-tenant
Great Bloghttps://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blogs-by-sap/go-for-your-quick-win-migrate-identity-provisioning-tenants-to-sap-cloud/ba-p/13536739
  

 

Migration Notice

You have received a message like below:

“Migration of your tenant to SAP Cloud Identity infrastructure is strongly encouraged. It brings significant advantages, such as new features and improved integration between SAP Cloud Identity Services.”

 

What does this means?: 

SAP is asking to migrate the IPS to the IAS infrastructure. The new SAP IAS panel holds inside an Identity Provisioning IPS. Doing this migration, IPS will not exist any more and everytihing will be managed from IAS.

The SAP IAS panel now integrates the SAP IPS panel into the same cockpit.

If your IAS looks like this, means you have NOT done the upgrade.

 

 Or, if your IPS look like this, also means the migration has not been done, and that it’s requiered.

Soon or later all the clients should have to do it. Don’t you worry, since is a easy process, but pay attention to the reccomendations below.

Once the migration is done the “Identity Provisoning” tab will appear.

 

Let’s get started!

Preparation

  • Ensure you have a bundle tenant running on SAP BTP. Standalone tenants cannot be migrated.
  • Ensure you have an admin user on the target IAS.
  • Ensure that no provisioning jobs are running before you start the migration. Stop manually triggered jobs and pause the scheduled ones.
  • If you use connectivity destination for any of your systems, do not modify them while the migration is running.

After migration, you will have access to your IPS tenant on Neo environment for 30 days. After that, the tenant is offboarded and cannot be restored. Although your Neo tenant will be available for 30 days, we recommend that you do not perform any operations on it, such as running jobs, adding provisioning systems and others.

Step by Step

 

  1. Click on Tenant Migration

 

The migration starting screen will appear:

 

Once you have selected the Source Systems and the Target ones, you can run the Wizard by clicking on the “Finish” button.

 

 

The Wizard is more or less fast.

 

Once the process is finished you will see a screenshot like below

 

After the Wizard has finished, all the source systems will still be enabled in IPS and disabled in IAS.

At the IAS panel > under Identity Provisioning  you will find the exact copy of target and source systems. The copied systemas are disabled and not scheduled.

 

The next step is to disable all the systems in IPS and enable in IAS. Run manually and check if everything goes ok.

 

Then you can consider the migration as finished!

entry.title: Secure SAP SuccessFactors APIs with SAP API Management and Microsoft Entra Token Exchange in IAS
entry.content:
April, 2026  |  SAP Integration Suite & Security

Introduction

Enterprise organizations frequently operate in multi-cloud environments where Microsoft Azure — and specifically Microsoft Entra ID (formerly Azure Active Directory) — serves as the primary identity provider. At the same time, SAP landscapes rely on SAP Cloud Identity Services – Identity Authentication (IAS) as the trusted token authority for SAP applications.

When these worlds need to meet at an API boundary — for example, a custom application or integration runtime managed in Azure that must consume SAP SuccessFactors APIs — a common question arises: how do you bridge identity without compromising security or creating brittle, hard-to-maintain workarounds?

This post walks through a standardized pattern that solves exactly this problem. Using SAP Integration Suite – API Management as the enforcement point, SAP Identity Authentication (IAS) as the canonical token issuer, and OAuth 2.0 JWT Bearer Token Exchange (RFC 7523) as the bridge mechanism, API consumers can present a native Microsoft Entra ID token and receive seamless, secure access to SAP SuccessFactors OData and REST APIs — with no custom identity mapping code required.

In scope: This pattern covers machine-to-machine (M2M) API access using the client_credentials grant type. The approach is equally applicable to service integrations, middleware, and automated data pipelines operating under a service principal identity registered in Microsoft Entra ID.

The Challenge: Two Identity Worlds, One API Gateway

SAP SuccessFactors enforces that API access tokens must be issued by a trusted identity provider. Historically, this meant developers either had to maintain separate credential sets within the SAP ecosystem or implement custom token-translation logic inside their integration middleware.

Neither approach scales well. Separate credentials introduce operational overhead and increase the attack surface. Custom token-exchange code is fragile, difficult to audit, and tightly coupled to specific implementation details.

The ideal solution standardizes identity at the API gateway layer, ensuring that:

  • API consumers authenticate against the identity provider they already trust — Microsoft Entra ID.
  • The gateway handles token exchange transparently, with no changes required in client applications.
  • SAP SuccessFactors receives only IAS-issued tokens, preserving SAP's trust model.
  • All token validation and exchange follows open, auditable standards (OAuth 2.0, OIDC, RFC 7523).

Solution Overview

The pattern uses SAP API Management as the central brokering point. Inbound API calls carry a Microsoft Entra ID JWT token. The API Management policy pipeline intercepts this token, exchanges it for an IAS-issued access token via a ServiceCallout policy, then forwards the IAS token to SAP SuccessFactors. The SuccessFactors API endpoint validates the IAS token and returns the requested data.

Component Role in the Pattern

Microsoft Entra IDIssues the initial JWT access token to the API consumer (service principal / app registration).
API ConsumerAny client application that holds an Entra ID token.
SAP API ManagementPerforms the JWT Bearer Token Exchange, and routes the call to SuccessFactors with the IAS token.
SAP Identity AuthenticationAccepts the Entra JWT, validates it, and issues an IAS access token scoped for SuccessFactors.
SAP SuccessFactorsTarget API. Accepts only IAS-issued tokens (as per SAP Note 3681224).

Architecture Diagram

Figure 1 — End-to-end authentication flow: API Consumer → Microsoft Entra ID → SAP API Management → SAP Cloud Identity Services (IAS) → SAP SuccessFactors

The diagram illustrates the trust relationships and communication paths:

  • Azure side: An app registration in Microsoft Entra ID issues access_token JWTs to the API consumer under the client_credentials grant type.
  • SAP BTP side: Within SAP Cloud  identity Service - Identity Authentication (IAS) an IAS application representing SAP API Management is configured with Entra ID as a trusted (corporate) identity provider. A dependency link maps this IAS application to the IAS SuccessFactors application, which holds the scopes for SF APIs.
  • SuccessFactors side: Create technical user with authorization (RBAC) according to the resources exposed in API. User Name should be the same as subject claim (Entra ID service principal). To avoid user claims in the IAS access token, the technical user should not be synchronized, created in IAS.

Step-by-Step Authentication Flow

  1. Client acquires a Microsoft Entra ID access token.
    The API consumer authenticates to Microsoft Entra ID using the client_credentials grant type and receives a signed JWT.
    # Token request to Microsoft Entra ID
POST https://login.microsoftonline.com/{tenant-id}/oauth2/v2.0/token

client_id     = {{ENTRAClientid}}
client_secret = {{ENTRASecret}}
grant_type    = client_credentials
scope         = {{scope}}
  2. Client calls the SAP API Management endpoint with the Entra token.
    The Entra JWT is presented in the standard Authorization: Bearer header. The API Management proxy receives the request and begins policy evaluation.
    # Inbound API call to SAP API Management
GET https://{apim-host}/api/{resource-path}
Authorization: Bearer {{EntraAccessToken}}
  3. SAP API Management performs JWT Bearer Token Exchange (RFC 7523) via IAS.
    A ServiceCallout policy in the API proxy calls the IAS token endpoint. The Entra token is passed as the assertion parameter. The resource parameter targets the IAS dependency that represents the SuccessFactors application.
    # Token exchange request from APIM to IAS
POST https://{ias-tenant}.accounts.ondemand.com/oauth2/token

client_id     = {{IASClientid}}
client_secret = {{IASSecret}}
grant_type    = urn:ietf:params:oauth:grant-type:jwt-bearer
assertion     = {{EntraAccessToken}}
resource      = urn:sap:identity:application:provider:name:{{dependency}}
  4. SAP IAS validates the Entra JWT and issues a SuccessFactors-scoped IAS token.
    IAS validates the inbound Entra JWT per RFC 7523 criteria (signature, issuer, audience, expiry). Because Microsoft Entra ID is configured as a trusted corporate identity provider in the IAS application, and the dependency link to the SuccessFactors IAS app is in place, IAS issues a new access token scoped for SuccessFactors. If validation fails, IAS returns an OAuth 2.0 error response (RFC 6749).
  5. SAP API Management forwards the call to SAP SuccessFactors with the IAS token.
    The API proxy replaces the inbound Entra token with the IAS access token and routes the request to the SuccessFactors OData or REST API endpoint. SuccessFactors validates the IAS token and returns the requested data.
    # Outbound API call from APIM to SuccessFactors
GET https://{sf-api-host}/odata/v2/{entity-set}
Authorization: Bearer {{IASAccessToken}}

Key Configuration Prerequisites

1. SAP Identity Authentication (IAS) — Corporate IdP Setup

In the IAS administration console, configure Microsoft Entra ID as a Corporate Identity Provider and set it as the Default Identity Provider under Conditional Authentication for the SAP API Management application. This establishes the trust chain that allows IAS to accept Entra-issued JWTs during the RFC 7523 exchange.

2. IAS Application — Dependency Configuration

Two IAS application registrations are required:

  • IAS SAP API Management app — This is the application that the ServiceCallout authenticates against. It must have
    • Grant Type: JWT Bearer
    • Trusted token issuer: Entra ID
    • Dependency: e.g. APIM2SF (pointing to the IAS SuccessFactors app)
  • IAS SuccessFactors app — Holds the OAuth scopes for SuccessFactors APIs and is referenced via the dependency link.

3. SAP SuccessFactors — Accept IAS-Issued Tokens

Important: SAP SuccessFactors must be explicitly configured to trust and accept IAS-issued tokens. This is a prerequisite documented in SAP Note 3681224. Review and apply part 1 of this note before deploying the pattern.

4. SAP API Management — Policy Configuration

The API proxy in SAP API Management must implement two policy steps:

  1. A ServiceCallout policy to call the IAS token endpoint with the RFC 7523 parameters described above.
  2. A Set Authorization: Bearer policy to replace the inbound Entra token with the IAS token before routing to SuccessFactors.

Why This Pattern Works — Key Design Principles

This design is effective because it respects the trust model of each platform without forcing either side to accept a foreign token directly:

  • No credential sharing across platforms. Client applications never need SAP-specific credentials. They authenticate exclusively with Entra ID using their existing service principal.
  • Standards-based token exchange. RFC 7523 is a well-specified IETF standard. Using it means the exchange logic is predictable, auditable, and interoperable.
  • Centralized policy enforcement. SAP API Management is the single enforcement and mediation point. Token exchange logic lives in the gateway policy, not scattered across individual client applications.
  • Transparent to API consumers. Client applications are completely unaware that a token exchange occurs. They present an Entra token and receive SuccessFactors data — the mediation is entirely internal to the gateway.
  • SAP's trust model is preserved. SuccessFactors only ever receives IAS-issued tokens, maintaining the integrity of the SAP identity trust chain.

Relevant Standards and References

Reference Description

RFC 7523JSON Web Token (JWT) Profile for OAuth 2.0 Client Authentication and Authorization Grants — defines the urn:ietf:params:oauth:grant-type:jwt-bearer grant type used for the token exchange.
RFC 6749The OAuth 2.0 Authorization Framework — baseline specification for token request/response formats, including error responses.
OIDCIdentity layer on top of OAuth 2.0. Microsoft Entra ID issues OIDC-compliant JWTs; IAS supports OIDC for trust federation.
SAP Note 3681224SAP Identity Authentication (IAS) configuration to enable generation of IAS-issued access tokens. Part 1 - Must be applied before this pattern can be used. Part 2  - Manage OIDC OAuth Client Application is not needed in this scenario!

Conclusion

The JWT Bearer Token Exchange pattern described here provides a clean, standards-based path for enterprises running Microsoft Entra ID as their primary identity provider to consume SAP SuccessFactors APIs through SAP API Management — without compromising on security, maintainability, or SAP's identity trust model.

By centralizing the token exchange in SAP API Management and leveraging the proven RFC 7523 grant type, organizations can:

  • Onboard new API consumers rapidly, using only their existing Entra ID service principal.
  • Restrict authorizations per API consumers (Entra ID service principal) via RBAC in SAP SuccessFactors. 
  • Enforce consistent security policies at a single gateway layer.
  • Avoid brittle, platform-specific identity workarounds.
  • Maintain a clear audit trail of token issuance and exchange across both identity systems.

If your organization is evaluating multi-cloud API security patterns for SAP workloads, this approach is worth considering as part of your broader integration suite strategy.

Related resources:
SAP Note 3681224 — SAP SuccessFactors: Enabling IAS token acceptance
SAP Cloud Identity Services – Identity Authentication documentation
SAP API Management documentation
IETF RFC 7523 — JWT Profile for OAuth 2.0

entry.title: Q1 2026 Quarterly Release Highlights: SAP BTP Security and Identity & Access Management
entry.content:
Another quarter done and dusted! We kicked off the new year with several new features and capabilities.

For a complete overview of feature deliveries for SAP Cloud Identity Services, check out our list of all new feature announcements in the SAP Cloud Identity Services Release Notes on the SAP Help Portal.

SAP Cloud Identity Services: Multiple corporate identity providers with the same issuer

In large enterprise environments, a single corporate identity provider often can't satisfy every trust requirement — especially when different business units have conflicting needs. Based on a customer improvement request, SAP Cloud Identity Services now supports multiple OpenID Connect corporate IdP configurations sharing the same issuer URL, giving teams the flexibility they need. For more information, see SAP Note 3718653 

SAP Cloud Identity Services: Authorization Management Service API for customer-specific policies

The Authorization Management (AMS) component of SAP Cloud Identity Services now offers an API that allows you to maintain customer-specific policies. This is a meaningful step toward automating governance and reducing manual overhead for security teams. For general information on AMS, please check the SAP Cloud Identity Services Developer Guide.  

SAP Custom Domain Service: New REST API for automatic certificate renewal

Managing domain certificates manually is error-prone — and with certificate lifespans soon set to drop to 90 days or less, automation becomes essential. The SAP Custom Domain Service now includes a REST API that handles certificate renewal automatically. It allows customers to build an integration with both on-premise and cloud-based Certificate Lifecycle Management processes. For details, please check the SAP Help Portal.

Stay in the loop

Join the SAP BTP Security and SAP Cloud Identity Services communities to get updates, share feedback, and connect with others!

entry.title: Configuring Single Sign-On (SSO) for SAP Datasphere using SAP Cloud Identity Services (IAS)
entry.content:
In today’s enterprise landscape, managing secure and seamless access across multiple applications is not just a convenience - it’s a necessity. As organizations expand their SAP ecosystem, implementing a centralized authentication mechanism becomes critical for both user experience and security.

In this blog, I will walk through in detail on how Single Sign-On (SSO) can be effectively implemented for SAP Datasphere using SAP Cloud Identity Services, specifically leveraging the SAP Identity Authentication Service, integrated with OKTA. Microsoft Active Directory integration follows similar procedure.

What is Single Sign-On (SSO)?

Single Sign-On (SSO) is an authentication mechanism that allows users to log in once and gain access to multiple applications without being prompted to log in again.

Instead of managing multiple credentials, users authenticate through a trusted identity provider, which securely handles access across integrated systems.

Key Objective:

  • One Identity
  • One Login
  • Multiple Secure Access Points

What is SAP Datasphere?

SAP Datasphere is SAP’s next-generation cloud-based data warehousing solution that enables organizations to unify, analyze, and manage data across landscapes.

It provides:

  • Seamless data integration from SAP and non-SAP sources
  • Advanced analytics and data modeling capabilities
  • Business-ready semantic layers

Given its critical role in enterprise data strategy, secure and efficient user access becomes essential.

What are SAP Cloud Identity Services?

SAP Cloud Identity Services is SAP’s central identity management platform designed to handle authentication, authorization, and identity lifecycle management across SAP and non-SAP applications.

It consists of two main components:

  • Identity Authentication Service (IAS)
  • Identity Provisioning Service (IPS)

This blog focuses on IAS as the authentication engine for SSO.

What is SAP Identity Authentication Service (IAS)?

SAP Identity Authentication Service is a cloud-based identity provider that manages user authentication and provides secure access to SAP applications.

It supports:

  • SAML 2.0, OAuth, OpenID Connect
  • Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA)
  • Conditional authentication policies

Architecture Overview: SSO with SAP IAS as Proxy

In this implementation:

  • IAS acts as a Proxy Identity Provider
  • Authentication requests are redirected to OKTA
  • IAS validates and passes authentication back to SAP Applications
  • Email address is used as the Unique User Identifier (Attribute)

Flow Simplified:

  1. User accesses SAP Datasphere
  2. Redirected to IAS (Proxy Identity Provider)
  3. IAS forwards authentication to OKTA
  4. User logs in via corporate credentials
  5. IAS validates and grants access
  6. User gains seamless access to SAP Datasphere

Why use Email as the User Attribute?

Using email as a unique identifier ensures:

  • Global uniqueness across systems
  • Alignment with corporate identity standards
  • Simplified user mapping across SAP and non-SAP applications

Benefits of Single Sign-On (SSO)

  1. Enhanced User Experience
  • Eliminates repeated logins
  • Faster access to applications
  • Reduced password fatigue

      2. Improved Security

  • Centralized authentication control
  • Enables Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA)
  • Reduces attack surface from password reuse
  1. Increased Productivity
  • Less time spent on login issues
  • Streamlined access to enterprise tools

End-to-End Configuration Steps

SAP IAS – OKTA Integration

Step 1: Extract the IAS Tenant Metadata: Login IAS as Administrator -> Applications & Resources -> Tenant Settings -> Single Sign-On -> SAML 2.0 Configuration -> Download Metadata File

Step 2: Configure IAS Tenant in OKTA: Login OKTA Admin Portal as Administrator -> Click on the 'Use single sign on' - 'Add App' option -> Create New App -> Platform = Web | Sign on method = SAML 2.0 -> Create

Provide IAS Tenant Description -> Maintain Single Sign-On Endpoint URL from IAS (ACS End Point) as Single Sign-On URL |  Recipient URL | Destination URL. Audience Restriction -> IAS Domain. Name ID Format -> E-mail Address. Digest Algorithm -> SHA256 -> Save.

Navigate to the 'Sign On' tab -> Click on 'Identity Provider metadata' hyperlink -> Export Configuration Metadata into an XML file

Step 3: Configure OKTA as Trusted Corporate Identity Provider : Login IAS Portal as Administrator -> Click on Identity Providers -> Corporate Identity Providers -> Create

Go to SAML 2.0 Configuration -> Browse and upload the OKTA Metadata XML file -> Save

Step 4: SAP Datasphere - IAS Configuration:

Only a System Owner can perform Single Sign-On Administration activity in SAP Datasphere.

Assign the System Owner Privilege with valid email address

Navigate to Information -> Administration

Step 4.1: Go to Security Tab -> Click on Change icon -> Authentication Method -> Choose SAML Single Sign-On (SSO)

Step 4.2: Under SAML Single Sign-On (SSO) Configuration -> Download Service Provider Metadata -> Extract SAP Datasphere Tenant’s Metadata (Here SAP Datasphere is the Service Provider)

We will complete parallel configuration in IAS for SAP Datasphere and resume further steps for seamless verification process

Step 4.2.1: Configure SAP Datasphere Application in IAS

Open new browser session -> Login IAS Tenant as Administrator -> Applications & Resources -> Applications -> Create -> Provide Display Name -> Choose Type as SAP Datasphere Solution -> Protocol Type – SAML2.0

Go to SAML 2.0 Configuration -> Upload SAP Datasphere Metadata (Service Provider) extracted from Step 4.2

Maintain Subject Name Identifier with Email as Primary Attribute. It is optional to configure fallback attribute.

Default Name ID Format -> Email

By default, Conditional Authentication will be set with Identity Authentication as Default Identity Provider

Resuming Configuration in SAP Datasphere -> Administrator -> Security

 Step 4.3: Under SAML Single Sign-On (SSO) Configuration -> Upload Identity Provider Metadata -> Upload IAS Tenant Metadata extracted from Step 1

Step 4.4: Under Confirm that the mapping is working, provide the System Owner E-mail Address -> Verify Account -> Follow instructions as per prompt

Note: System Owner ID must exist in IAS with correct e-mail address maintained

Step 4.5: Click Save icon to enable SAML Single Sign-On -> Convert

Authentication Verification with IAS: Try login SAP Datasphere Tenant in an Incognito / Private Browser session. Authentication to happen by IAS.

 Enabling IAS as Proxy:

Go to Conditional Authentication of SAP Datasphere Application and change the Default Identity Provider to the OKTA Corporate Identity Provider created in Step 3.

Authentication Verification with IAS as Proxy : Try login SAP Datasphere Tenant in an Incognito / Private Browser session. Authentication to happen by OKTA via IAS.

Conclusion

Implementing SSO for SAP Datasphere using SAP Cloud Identity Services - SAP Identity Authentication Service provides a robust, scalable, and secure authentication framework.

By configuring IAS as Proxy for Corporate Identity Provider, organizations can achieve a unified authentication strategy that enhances both user experience and security posture.

Author’s Note

This implementation reflects a real-world enterprise approach to consolidating authentication mechanisms across SAP landscapes using modern identity services.

If you're planning your Single Sign-On (SSO) journey or optimizing SAP Applications Architecture, this model serves as a strong foundation.

Referral Links

  • https://me.sap.com/notes/0002943651
  • https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/connect-okta-to-identity-authentication/ba-p/13468665

#SAP #SingleSignOn #SSO #SAPDatasphere #SAPCloudIdentityServices #SAPIdentityAuthenticationService #SAPIAS #OKTA
